metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the LLM provider call failed: Connection error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `_backend.py` the mechanism by which a backend advertises optional capabilities (e.g. 'structural subtyping: `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)` using `runtime_checkable` Protocols' or 'explicit `capabilities` frozenset property on `Backend`'), and document this in the Approach section so the contract suite and both implementations are consistent.
evidence: 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links, not via structural introspection' — leaves the positive detection mechanism undefined
The three capability Protocols are named but no field/method for capability advertisement is specified in the Backend facade description
findings.finding: The plan describes capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) as something a backend 'advertises,' and the contract suite is supposed to test them 'via behavior,' but the mechanism by which a backend signals it implements a capability is never specified (duck-typing via `hasattr`/`isinstance`, an explicit `capabilities: frozenset` property, ABC registration, etc.). This is a design decision that directly affects both `JiraBackend` and `FakeBackend` implementations and how the contract suite branches on it — an executor cannot implement consistently without choosing.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior)' + Testing section same phrase
scenarios: JiraBackend author uses `isinstance` checks; FakeBackend author uses a `capabilities` property; contract suite uses `hasattr` — three inconsistent approaches, all plausible from the plan text
Contract suite cannot be written without deciding how to detect capability, so the executor must make an undocumented design choice
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Capability detection: mark the three capability Protocols with `@runtime_checkable`; the reconciler (and contract suite) uses `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)` — no explicit advertisement property needed. `JiraBackend` inherits all three; `FakeBackend` inherits only the ones under test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cardinality-proving command to AC2, e.g. `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py --collect-only -q | grep -c 'backend_factory'` asserting the count equals 2× the number of contract test functions, so that both parametrize arms are confirmed to have collected and run.
evidence: No count assertion (e.g. `pytest --collect-only | grep test_backend_contract | wc -l` asserting ≥ 2×N tests) is specified
'make test' is a necessary but not sufficient proving command for the 'both backends' claim — defect type 5 (missing cardinality assertion)
findings.finding: AC2 says the contract suite is 'verified by `make test` running the suite twice via the `backend_factory` fixture,' but there is no proving command that confirms the suite actually executed against BOTH backends (not just the first parametrize value). If the `FakeBackend` factory fixture is misconfigured and silently skipped, `make test` still exits 0 and AC2 appears satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'JiraBackend implements Backend; the contract suite passes when parameterized over both JiraBackend … and FakeBackend — verified by make test running the suite twice via the backend_factory fixture'
scenarios: FakeBackend fixture is accidentally omitted from the parametrize list; make test exits 0; AC2 is signed off; the contract suite never ran against FakeBackend
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'additionally verified by `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py --collect-only -q | grep -c "\[jira\]\|\[fake\]"` asserting the count equals 2 × (number of contract test functions).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit baseline-capture step to the Testing section, e.g. 'Before implementing JiraBackend, run `python -m pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py --snapshot-update` (or equivalent `json.dump` script) against the current `main` to record fixture files; commit them; then implement JiraBackend and confirm tests pass without `--snapshot-update`.'
evidence: No baseline-capture step or command is specified in Approach or Testing sections
The phrase 'captured from current behavior' describes what the fixtures ARE, not HOW to produce them reproducibly
findings.finding: AC3 requires characterization tests to assert 'byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main' outputs captured as 'literal expected values,' but the plan provides no step or command for generating that baseline — no instruction to run the mappers against `main` and record the fixture snapshots before implementing `JiraBackend`. An executor who skips this step (or writes expected values by hand) satisfies the AC text without a true regression guarantee.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main … verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
scenarios: Executor writes expected values by hand, introducing accidental normalization differences that the characterization test then encodes as 'correct' — the tests pass but don't actually pin pre-story behavior

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that explicitly requires the Teeth mutation step: a deliberate delegation perturbation (e.g. dropping the priority map) must cause at least one contract or characterization test to go RED, with the commit or test-run output as evidence.
evidence: 'Teeth: perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore.'
ABSENCE: no AC text references this mutation/perturbation step or requires it to be demonstrated.
findings.finding: The 'Teeth' mutation test described in the Testing section has no covering acceptance criterion, leaving a described deliverable unverifiable at story close.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Teeth' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: Implementer skips the Teeth step (since no AC gates on it); the contract suite passes without ever being proven to catch a real delegation bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Demonstrated Teeth: a one-line perturbation of JiraBackend delegation (e.g. removing priority from the outbound map) causes ≥1 contract or characterization test to fail; the perturbation is then reverted and `make test` is green — evidenced by a before/after test-run diff in the PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which tests must be seen RED first: restrict the 'RED-first / TDD-held-out' directive explicitly to the contract suite (which tests the not-yet-existing Backend/JiraBackend interface), and state separately that characterization tests over existing pure functions will be GREEN when authored but serve as regression pins — resolving the contradiction between 'seen RED first' and 'pure functions already exist'.
evidence: 'TDD-held-out: the contract + characterization tests are authored and seen RED first, then JiraBackend is implemented to green.'
'The mapping contracts assert against the pure mapper functions (_map_local_to_jira_fields, adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit, inbound extraction), which need no transport at all.'
'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs of the pure Jira mappers … for representative tickets, proving JiraBackend delegation is behavior-preserving.'
findings.finding: The plan states characterization tests 'are authored and seen RED first' (Approach/Testing), but those tests target existing pure mapper functions that already exist and would be GREEN immediately upon authoring — producing a cross-section contradiction with the RED-first / TDD-held-out directive.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TDD-held-out' note; Testing section — 'Tier: unit + contract, RED-first'; Testing section — 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs of the pure Jira mappers (_map_local_to_jira_fields …) … which need no transport at all'
scenarios: Implementer cannot comply with RED-first for characterization tests without a contrived workaround; this creates ambiguity about which tests must be held out and seen RED, risking the TDD discipline being abandoned for all tests.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, split the directive: 'Contract suite tests are authored and seen RED first (Backend/JiraBackend do not exist yet). Characterization tests over the pure mapper functions will be GREEN when authored (functions already exist); their role is regression pinning, not TDD held-out.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc/ADR update step: either extend ADR 0035 with the finalized Protocol signatures and RemoteRef semantics, or author a short new ADR/design-doc section capturing the Backend facade contract, so future backend implementers have a normative reference.
evidence: Plan references 'ADR 0035 §(d) [rebar:ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb]' as motivation but specifies no ADR update step
Edit-set lists only _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, and test files — no doc/ADR file
ABSENCE: no mention of updating AGENTS.md, ADR 0035, or creating a new ADR for the finalized Protocol shapes
The five role Protocols and three capability Protocols are new architectural facts that downstream backend authors must implement
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant new architectural contract (8 Protocols, the Backend facade, RemoteRef identity type, and an adapter contract test pattern) but does not specify updating ADR 0035 or authoring a new ADR/design-doc entry capturing the finalized Protocol signatures and their rationale. Future implementers of a second backend must discover the contract solely from source code.
findings.location: Context / Problem (ADR 0035 reference); Edit-set (_backend.py, JiraBackend); Scope section
scenarios: A developer implementing a second backend (epic be74) has no normative doc telling them which Protocols to implement, what the round-trip contract guarantees, or how RemoteRef is expected to be used
ADR 0035 references §(d) for RemoteRef but the concrete fields (vendor, instance, remote_id) and their semantics are not recorded anywhere the plan touches
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Edit-set: 'Update ADR 0035 §(d) (or add a new §) to record the five role Protocols, three capability Protocols, their method signatures, and the RemoteRef(vendor, instance, remote_id) semantics. Cross-link from AGENTS.md if it tracks adapter extension points.' This can be a concise prose addition, not a full new ADR, since the story is an elaboration of an existing decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or augment the self-authored-oracle characterization approach: either (a) derive expected values from an independent source (a manually-verified fixture, the ADR/spec, or a reference Jira API response captured and human-reviewed before the story starts), or (b) add an explicit note in the AC that a named human reviewer has signed off on the captured baselines as known-correct, so the test is not a pure change-detector.
evidence: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty).'
The oracle is derived from the SUT's own current output with no independent verification step described.
findings.finding: The characterization tests are described as capturing literal expected values 'from current behavior' of the pure Jira mappers. This is a self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: if the current mapper output is wrong, the test will enshrine the bug and remain green. There is no independent oracle (human-verified fixture, spec-derived golden dataset, or reference document) anchoring the expected values.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs … verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior'; AC line 3 ('byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior')
scenarios: A latent bug in _map_local_to_jira_fields or inbound extraction is captured as the expected value; all subsequent tests pass green while the bug is locked in and future fixes are flagged as regressions.
A reviewer cannot distinguish 'correct behavior' from 'current behavior' because no independent source of truth is cited.
findings.suggested_fix: Before running the capture step, produce a small set of representative input fixtures whose expected outputs are derived from the Jira API spec or manually verified against a real (or recorded) Jira response. Store these as static YAML/JSON golden files committed to the repo. The characterization tests then assert against these pre-verified fixtures rather than against the live SUT's current output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the capability Protocol contract tests to structural/protocol checks only (e.g., assert `isinstance(JiraBackend(), SupportsLinks)` and that the capability methods are callable with the right signatures), deferring the behavioral 'never asked when not advertised' assertion to S4 where the dispatch mechanism is built.
evidence: Search for 'SupportsLinks', 'SupportsComments', 'SupportsIncremental' returns zero hits across the entire codebase — no capability-gating dispatch exists anywhere.
The core always syncs links (outbound_links.py is unconditionally imported by outbound_differ.py:74) and always syncs comments (outbound_comments.py:46-51) — there is no conditional capability check.
S4 ticket (6673-7636-a116-4f90) is where core consumers are rewired through the Backend port; capability-dispatch logic would land there, not in S2.
Plan's own Scope section: 'Out of scope: rewiring any core call site or construction site (S4).'
findings.finding: The contract-suite Testing section specifies a test that asserts 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior', but the capability-dispatch mechanism that would enforce this does not exist in the current core and is explicitly deferred to S4. Writing this test in S2 either produces a vacuous assertion (always passes because no dispatch logic exists to violate) or requires smuggling capability-gating logic into the test harness that is out-of-scope for this story. This is premature: the test gates behavior that no current call site exhibits.
findings.location: Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links)'
scenarios: The contract test for SupportsLinks-gating is written as RED-first but can never go RED (no dispatch mechanism exists to violate), so the TDD discipline is broken — the test passes immediately without any implementation.
Alternatively, the test author implements a capability-dispatch shim inside the FakeBackend or test harness to make the test meaningful, inadvertently landing S4-scope logic in the S2 test layer.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the capability-gating behavioral assertion with: (a) a structural test that `JiraBackend` satisfies all three capability Protocols, (b) a negative structural test that `FakeBackend` without the mixin does NOT satisfy them, and (c) a TODO comment citing S4 for the dispatch-level behavioral contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether SupportsIncremental has a concrete consumer or implementation story in the current epic scope; if not, defer it to the story that actually builds incremental sync (or the second-backend epic `be74`) rather than pinning its signature now.
evidence: Search for 'SupportsIncremental' returns zero hits in the codebase.
Search for 'incremental' in the reconciler source returns zero hits related to incremental sync.
ADR ticket ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb lists SupportsIncremental in the §(d) Protocol table, but the ADR is a docs-only story; 'mentioned in the ADR' does not create a concrete consumer.
Epic tickets S2–S5 (ef33, ce4d implied by S3/S4 refs, 94f9, 6673, dfb9) have no story that implements incremental sync — the feature has no done-definition in the current epic scope.
SupportsLinks wraps outbound_links.py (real code); SupportsComments wraps outbound_comments.py (real code); SupportsIncremental wraps nothing.
findings.finding: SupportsIncremental names a capability Protocol for incremental sync, but incremental sync has zero existing implementation in the codebase — no current call site, no current mechanism, and no story in the epic that builds it before a second backend arrives. Unlike SupportsLinks and SupportsComments (which wrap real existing code paths), SupportsIncremental is a forward slot for a feature that does not exist, making it the one Protocol that fails YAGNI's 'serves a current done-definition' test.
findings.location: Context / Problem section: 'the three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental)'; Acceptance Criteria AC1.
scenarios: JiraBackend implements SupportsIncremental with stub/placeholder methods that are never called, adding dead code to the production adapter from day one.
A future implementer treats the stubbed SupportsIncremental as a specification contract and builds to it, even though the design was never validated against a real use case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `TicketTransport.transition_*` should be derived from `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name` (acli.py:481, the REST-based instance method), not the module-level free function `transition_issue` at acli.py:122, and document the resolved method name explicitly in the Protocol derivation.
evidence: acli.py:122 — module-level `def transition_issue(jira_key, status)` (free function, constructs its own AcliClient)
acli.py:481 — `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(self, jira_key, target_status)` (the actual instance method)
The plan's wording 'transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient' is ambiguous; if read as `transition_issue`, that name does not exist on the class — only `transition_issue_by_name` does
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient', implying a method named `transition_issue` exists on AcliClient. In reality, the AcliClient instance-level transition method is `transition_issue_by_name` (acli.py:481, REST-based); `transition_issue` (acli.py:122) is a module-level free function that constructs its own AcliClient internally. Using the wrong name in the TicketTransport Protocol would produce a mismatch that the JiraBackend delegation shim could not satisfy.
findings.location: Approach section: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
scenarios: TicketTransport Protocol defines `transition_issue(...)` — JiraBackend can't satisfy it because AcliClient has no such instance method
TicketTransport Protocol defines `transition_issue_by_name(...)` — plan text is misleading but implementation lands correctly; characterization tests would pass anyway
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: '`TicketTransport` ← `AcliClient.create_issue`, `AcliClient.update_issue`, `AcliClient.get_issue`, `AcliClient.add_comment`, `AcliClient.search_issues`, `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name` (the REST-based instance method at acli.py:481; the module-level `transition_issue` free function is NOT an AcliClient method and should not appear in the Protocol).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contract test file path: confirm it is `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py` (consistent with the existing test hierarchy) and update the ticket's `file_impact` entry to match.
evidence: Plan body (Testing section): 'tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py'
Ticket file_impact entry: '{"path": "tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py", ...}'
All existing test files confirmed under tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ via grep (test_applier_mapping.py, test_decision_census.py, etc.)
findings.finding: The ticket's `file_impact` record names the contract test file as `tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py`, but the plan body consistently refers to `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py`. The existing test hierarchy (confirmed by grep across dozens of files) lives under `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/`. These are different filesystem paths; a `make test` run that discovers tests by path would find one or neither depending on which path is actually used.
findings.location: Testing section ('tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py') vs. ticket file_impact ('tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py')
scenarios: Implementer follows file_impact and creates tests/reconciler/test_backend_contract.py — test runner configured for tests/unit/ never picks it up; AC 'make test running the suite twice' cannot be verified
Implementer follows plan body and creates tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py — file_impact is wrong and any automation reading it points to a nonexistent file
findings.suggested_fix: Update file_impact to `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py` to match the plan body and the actual test directory layout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete modules `JiraBackend.IdentityConvention` delegates to for the stamp (add_label + set_entity_property in `dispatch_one.py` / `apply_inbound_records.py`) and find (`_has_rebar_id_label` in `binding_walk.py`, JQL search in `dispatch_one.py`) operations, or acknowledge that these are extracted into the Protocol's method signatures without specifying delegation sources.
evidence: dispatch_one.py:237: `jql = f'labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"'` / dispatch_one.py:320: `_call_with_retry(client.add_label, jira_key, f'rebar-id:{local_id}')` / dispatch_one.py:321: `_call_with_retry(client.set_entity_property, ...)` — stamp path
binding_walk.py:320: `def _has_rebar_id_label(fields: ...) -> bool:` — find path
apply_inbound_records.py:223: `_call_with_retry(client.add_label, jira_key, f'rebar-id:{local_id}')` — inbound stamp
binding_store.py:516/522/526: recovery search via colon+hyphen forms
None of these four modules are named in the Approach section as delegation sources for IdentityConvention
findings.finding: The plan's `IdentityConvention` Protocol source is unnamed at the module level. The rebar-id stamp+find logic the Protocol must abstract is spread across at least four modules outside the adapter dir — `dispatch_one.py` (JQL dedup search + `client.add_label` + `client.set_entity_property`), `binding_walk.py` (`_has_rebar_id_label`), `apply_inbound_records.py` (inbound stamp), and `binding_store.py` (recovery search) — none of which are cited as delegation sources for `JiraBackend`'s `IdentityConvention` implementation.
findings.location: Approach section — 'IdentityConvention ← the `rebar-id` label read/write convention, expressed over `RemoteRef`'
scenarios: Implementer of `JiraBackend.identity_convention` has no guidance on which modules to delegate to, risking a partial or incorrect implementation (e.g., implementing only the label-write but omitting the JQL-search-by-label 'find' half).
The contract test for `IdentityConvention.stamp_and_find` may be written against a surface that doesn't exist yet in the delegated modules, causing RED that can't be greened by delegation alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `TicketTransport` method list to explicitly include `add_label`, `remove_label`, `set_entity_property`, `get_entity_property`, `search_issues`, and `delete_issue` (or state which Protocol each belongs to), since all are called on the `AcliClient` in the identity-stamp and dedup paths.
evidence: dispatch_one.py:320: `client.add_label(...)` called in the outbound-create path
dispatch_one.py:321: `client.set_entity_property(...)` called in the outbound-create path
dispatch_one.py:238: `client.search_issues(jql)` called for JQL dedup
dispatch_one.py:326: `client.delete_issue(jira_key)` called in rollback
binding_store.py:516: `client.add_label(...)` called in recovery
apply_inbound_records.py:223: `client.add_label(...)` called in inbound create
Plan names only 'create/read/update/transition/comment' for TicketTransport — no mention of add_label, remove_label, set_entity_property, search_issues, delete_issue, get_entity_property
findings.finding: The plan's `TicketTransport` method set is underspecified. The Approach names only 'the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`', but `AcliClient` also exposes `add_label`, `remove_label`, `set_entity_property`, `get_entity_property`, `search_issues` (JQL), and `delete_issue` — all of which are called in the rebar-id stamp/dedup/rollback path (`dispatch_one.py`) and the inbound create path. It is unspecified whether these belong to `TicketTransport` or to `IdentityConvention`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on `acli.AcliClient`'
scenarios: A `FakeBackend` implementing only the five named methods will fail contract tests that exercise the dedup JQL path or identity-stamp path, since those paths call `search_issues`, `add_label`, `set_entity_property`.
The `JiraBackend` author may define a `TicketTransport` Protocol that omits these methods, breaking structural compatibility with the full `AcliClient` surface.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, expand: 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods AND identity-transport methods (add_label, remove_label, set_entity_property, get_entity_property, search_issues, delete_issue) on `acli.AcliClient`', OR assign the latter group to `IdentityConvention`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Qualify the round-trip contract assertion in the Testing section: specify which fields are included (e.g. title, description, priority, assignee) and explicitly call out which fields are intentionally excluded from round-trip symmetry (status — lossy Jira workflow mapping; ticket_type — excluded by bug 36af sync exception; parent — conditional on binding_store) so the contract tests are grounded and the RED phase is a clean failing assertion, not an accidental or structural failure.
evidence: outbound_fields.py _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_STATUS maps 'blocked'→'In Progress' with no inverse
inbound_differ.py:143-158 explicitly excludes ticket_type from the inbound diff field_map (bug 36af)
inbound_differ.py:166-171 uses shape-tolerant assignee matching, not identity
Plan: 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields the reconciler syncs' — no field-scope qualification
findings.finding: The contract suite specifies 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields the reconciler syncs' without acknowledging that the outbound and inbound mappers are intentionally non-symmetric inverses. In the actual code: `status` is lossy (e.g. 'blocked'→'In Progress' has no reverse), `ticket_type` is excluded from the inbound diff (bug 36af), and `assignee` involves dict↔string shape normalization. A naive round-trip assertion will either go RED for legitimate reasons (requiring the contract to be silently narrowed post-RED), or it will be written so narrowly that it gives false confidence. Either outcome undermines the 'TDD RED-first' guarantee the plan commits to.
findings.location: Testing section: 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields the reconciler syncs'
scenarios: Contract test for 'status' round-trip: outbound maps 'blocked'→'In Progress', inbound maps 'In Progress'→'open' (no 'blocked' recovery without rebar-status label); round-trip fails for status=blocked, causing a confusing RED.

Contract test for 'ticket_type': outbound maps 'bug'→'Bug', but inbound deliberately does not sync ticket_type back (bug 36af); the round-trip never closes, making the test either wrong or trivially vacuous.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the contract to: 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same value for the symmetric fields (title, description, priority); status, ticket_type, and parent are explicitly excluded from round-trip assertions and are instead validated by the characterization tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach section how OutboundMapper's Protocol method signature handles the `binding_store`, `local_ticket_types`, and `emit_detach_clear` parameters from `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — either include them in the Protocol (document that FakeBackend stubs them) or describe how JiraBackend's delegation shim will supply them from context, so the implementer can define a stable signature in `_backend.py` without revisiting it in S4.
evidence: outbound_fields.py:112-116 _map_local_to_jira_fields signature: def _map_local_to_jira_fields(ticket, binding_store=None, local_ticket_types=None, emit_detach_clear=False)
Plan only says 'OutboundMapper ← _map_local_to_jira_fields' with no mention of how the extra parameters are threaded into the Protocol
binding_store is a reconciler-core object (BindingStoreProtocol already defined in outbound_differ.py) — its presence in the mapper Protocol is a seam-boundary decision
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `OutboundMapper`'s method signature handles the `binding_store`, `local_ticket_types`, and `emit_detach_clear` parameters that `_map_local_to_jira_fields` requires. These are reconciler-core concerns threaded into the mapper (parent resolution, parent-type hierarchy check, parent-detach sentinel). If the Protocol method includes these parameters, every backend must accept them (including FakeBackend). If they are excluded, JiraBackend cannot faithfully delegate without a different calling convention. This design decision is load-bearing for both the Protocol definition and the FakeBackend implementation, yet the plan is silent on it.
findings.location: Approach section: 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
scenarios: Implementer defines OutboundMapper.map(ticket) without binding_store; JiraBackend.map() calls _map_local_to_jira_fields(ticket) and silently drops parent resolution — tests pass but parent sync is broken.
Implementer includes binding_store in OutboundMapper.map signature; FakeBackend contract test must now supply a BindingStoreProtocol stub, adding complexity not described in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ticket-graph parent: re-parent this leaf (S2) directly under the epic (bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a) rather than under S1 (ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb), since S1 is docs-only and the epic explicitly scopes this code work as S2.
evidence: Leaf delivers: new `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py`, new `adapters/jira/backend.py`, new `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py`, FakeBackend
Parent S1 scope states: 'No code changes.'
Parent S1 AC4: 'make lint passes on the branch (docs-only change introduces no lint error)'
Parent S1 file_impact contains only: 'docs/adr/0035-reconciler-vendor-adapter-seam.md'
Epic bbf1 decomposition correctly identifies this work as S2 (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798), a sibling of S1, both children of the epic — not a child of S1
findings.finding: The leaf (S2) delivers code artifacts (_backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, test suite with FakeBackend) that fall entirely outside its declared parent S1's scope. S1 is explicitly docs-only ('No code changes.' in its Scope section; its only AC is that 'make lint passes on the branch (docs-only change)'). Code deliverables are not a consistent narrowing of a docs-only parent — they are outside it.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section; parent S1 (ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb) Scope: 'No code changes.'
scenarios: Code review / merge gate: this leaf's code deliverables have no authorization in its declared parent's contract, meaning the parent's plan-review attestation does not cover them.
If the ticket graph is taken as authoritative, the leaf's scope is entirely divergent from its parent — every code file it touches is outside S1's approved surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the leaf's `parent_id` from `ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb` (S1, docs-only) to `bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a` (the epic), which already contains the exact scope this leaf implements in its S2 decomposition entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `RemoteRef` should be a `NamedTuple` (matching the existing repo prior art in `invariants.py`) or a `@dataclass` with an explicit `sys.modules` pre-registration guard — and document the chosen approach in `_backend.py`'s module docstring, consistent with the pattern already used in `outbound_differ.py`, `mutation.py`, and `invariants.py`.
evidence: Plan: 'the `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` dataclass'

invariants.py:107-112: 'A NamedTuple (not a dataclass) deliberately: invariants.py is exec'd standalone in tests via spec_from_file_location WITHOUT registering the module in sys.modules, and on Python 3.14 @dataclass resolves stringified (PEP 563) annotations through sys.modules[cls.__module__] at class-definition time, which raises AttributeError for such an unregistered module.'
outbound_fields.py:36, outbound_differ.py:213, inbound_translate.py:299 — all document the same spec_from_file_location standalone-load hazard with explicit sys.modules pre-registration guards

Plan Testing section: 'characterization tests pin the exact current outputs of the pure Jira mappers (_map_local_to_jira_fields, adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit, inbound extraction)' — these mappers are all loaded via spec_from_file_location in existing tests
findings.finding: The plan declares `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` as a `dataclass` in `_backend.py`, but the codebase documents a Python 3.14 hazard (invariants.py:107-112) that `@dataclass` raises `AttributeError` when a module is exec'd via `spec_from_file_location` without prior `sys.modules` registration — the exact load path used by the characterization tests over the pure Jira mappers. The plan does not address whether `_backend.py` needs the `sys.modules` pre-registration guard, or whether `RemoteRef` should be `NamedTuple` (the prior-art solution the repo already uses for this same problem).
findings.location: Approach section / Edit-set: `_backend.py` (`RemoteRef` dataclass) + Testing section (characterization tests over pure Jira mappers)
scenarios: A characterization test loads outbound_fields.py via spec_from_file_location; outbound_fields.py imports from _backend.py (e.g. for RemoteRef); _backend.py is not registered in sys.modules before exec; Python 3.14 @dataclass annotation resolution raises AttributeError — test suite fails on Python 3.14.
Even if _backend.py is not directly imported by mapper modules, future tests exec _backend.py itself standalone and hit the same trap, requiring a retrofit.
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `RemoteRef` as a `NamedTuple` (e.g. `class RemoteRef(NamedTuple): vendor: str; instance: str; remote_id: str`) — this is the solution the repo already documents for the Python 3.14 dataclass/spec_from_file_location hazard, requires no guard code, and is immutable/hashable by construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `_map_local_to_jira_fields`'s core-context parameters (`binding_store`, `local_ticket_types`, `emit_detach_clear`) are handled in the `OutboundMapper` Protocol signature — either include them (and name their types) or explain the wrapping strategy that preserves behavior without leaking core context.
evidence: outbound_fields.py:112: def _map_local_to_jira_fields(ticket, binding_store=None, local_ticket_types=None, emit_detach_clear=False)
Plan states 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls' and 'JiraBackend is a pure delegation shim and no logic moves yet'
Partially-verified: the function exists and is correctly named, but its multi-parameter signature creates an interface design gap the plan does not resolve
apply_inbound.py:492 calls _map_local_to_jira_fields with ticket + binding_store; outbound_differ.py:604 calls it with ticket + binding_store + local_ticket_types
findings.finding: _map_local_to_jira_fields — the function the plan assigns to the OutboundMapper Protocol — takes core-context parameters (binding_store, local_ticket_types, emit_detach_clear) that are owned by the reconciler core, not by the adapter. The plan says JiraBackend is 'a pure delegation shim' with 'zero behavior change,' but does not specify whether these parameters appear in the OutboundMapper Protocol signature or are elided, creating an unresolved capability gap: if they're in the signature the Protocol leaks core context; if they're absent the shim cannot faithfully delegate without altering call semantics.
findings.location: Approach section: 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
scenarios: OutboundMapper.map() method in the Protocol needs a concrete signature; if it omits binding_store the JiraBackend shim silently drops parent-resolution behavior (behavior change); if it includes binding_store the Protocol exposes a core-internal type.

The contract suite round-trip test ('outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips to the same local fields') would need to decide whether to supply a binding_store stub, but the plan does not describe this fixture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the abstract method signatures for each capability Protocol (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) in the plan or a referenced spec, so the contract suite and `JiraBackend` can be authored TDD-RED-first without guessing the interface.
evidence: Plan: 'the three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental)'
Plan: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it'
ADR §(d) (ticket ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb) only lists the names, never the method signatures
grep for 'SupportsLinks', 'SupportsComments', 'SupportsIncremental' returns zero matches in the entire repo — these Protocols do not exist yet and their method vocabulary is unspecified
findings.finding: The three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are named throughout the plan and contract suite but their method vocabulary — the abstract methods an implementer must provide — is never enumerated anywhere in the plan text or the upstream ADR §(d).
findings.location: Approach section ('three opt-in capability Protocols'), Testing section ('a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it'), AC line 1
scenarios: An implementer building JiraBackend cannot know which methods to implement on SupportsLinks (e.g. sync_links? get_links? add_link?) — the contract suite cannot go RED/GREEN without defined method names
FakeBackend author cannot implement the capability Protocols without knowing their method signatures
findings.suggested_fix: Add a method-signature table to the plan (or to a new `_backend.py` stub section): e.g. `SupportsLinks.sync_links(transport, local_id, links) -> None`, `SupportsComments.sync_comments(transport, local_id, comments) -> None`, `SupportsIncremental.fetch_since(transport, since: datetime) -> list[dict]`. Derive signatures from the existing `outbound_links.py`, `outbound_comments.py`, and fetcher call-sites the same way the role Protocols are derived.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at least the two methods implied by the contract test for `IdentityConvention` (e.g. `stamp(local_id: str) -> str` producing the label value, and `find(labels: Iterable[str]) -> RemoteRef | None`) so the contract test can be authored TDD-RED-first.
evidence: Plan: 'IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef'
Testing: 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
grep for 'IdentityConvention' returns zero matches in repo — Protocol does not exist
The contract test verbs 'stamps' and 'finds' imply at least two methods, but neither is named
findings.finding: IdentityConvention's method vocabulary is entirely unspecified: the plan describes it only as 'the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef,' but names no abstract methods, leaving the AC import test ('each name resolves') and the contract test ('IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef') ungroundable.
findings.location: Approach section ('IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention'), Testing section ('IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'), AC line 1
scenarios: JiraBackend implementer must invent method names (stamp_ref? make_label? find_ref? extract_ref?) without a spec — the contract test cannot be authored RED-first without knowing the method signatures
A second backend implementer needs IdentityConvention's full surface to implement the rebar-id convention over a non-label mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Derive signatures from binding_walk.py lines 341-341 (`rebar-id:` / `rebar-id-` prefix logic) and dispatch_one.py line 237 (`f'labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"'`). A minimal spec: `stamp(local_id: str) -> str` (returns `'rebar-id:{local_id}'`) and `find(labels: Iterable[str]) -> RemoteRef | None` (returns a RemoteRef when a matching label is present, else None).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the capability-advertise mechanism in the plan (e.g. a `Backend.capabilities() -> frozenset[type]` method, or a `supported_capabilities: tuple[type, ...]` attribute, or explicit `has_links: bool` flags) so the contract test and JiraBackend implementation can be authored unambiguously.
evidence: Plan quote: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it... not via structural introspection'
If not isinstance() / hasattr() checks, the advertise mechanism must be a method call — but no such method is defined on Backend or any capability Protocol in the plan
The Approach section defines _backend.py's contents but does not include a 'supports_capability()' or similar advertise method on Backend
findings.finding: The capability-advertise dispatch rule — 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links, observed via behavior not structural introspection' — is described as a contract test oracle but the mechanism by which the core detects or skips unsupported capabilities is never specified, leaving its placement in _backend.py, JiraBackend, or the core ambiguous.
findings.location: Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'
scenarios: FakeBackend does not implement SupportsLinks — the contract suite needs to verify the core never calls link-sync methods on it, but without knowing the advertise mechanism the test cannot set up the correct expectation
JiraBackend must advertise which capabilities it supports — but the plan gives no mechanism for this (return self from a capability property? implement the Protocol structurally? call a method?)

findings.finding: The plan asserts the contract suite will run JiraBackend 'over the existing Jira subprocess/REST fakes', but no such fakes exist. The only fake infrastructure found (tests/mocks/jira-cassette-loader.py) explicitly self-describes as a 'scaffold' with 'Live recording of cassettes from a real Jira tenant is a multi-day follow-up', and no recorded cassettes exist anywhere in the test tree. The plan's premise that there is usable Jira-subprocess or REST fake infrastructure to exercise JiraBackend is unverifiable and likely false.
findings.location: Approach section: 'run it against both JiraBackend (over the existing Jira subprocess/REST fakes) and FakeBackend'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, replace 'over the existing Jira subprocess/REST fakes' with a concrete description of what fake transport will be used (e.g., a new in-story AcliClient stub that records/replays calls, or an extension of the cassette-loader scaffold with the minimum cassettes needed for the contract assertions). Alternatively, constrain the JiraBackend suite leg to the subset of contract assertions that can be driven through pure-Python mapper calls (no subprocess) and explicitly note that transport-level contract coverage is deferred.

findings.finding: The plan states the contract suite will run `JiraBackend` 'over the existing Jira subprocess/REST fakes,' but no such fakes exist in the codebase. The only test infrastructure found (`tests/mocks/jira-cassette-loader.py`) self-describes as a 'scaffold only' with live cassette recording deferred to a future follow-up, and no recorded cassettes exist. There is no fallback specified for the case where the fakes are absent or incomplete.
findings.location: Approach section — 'run it against both JiraBackend (over the existing Jira subprocess/REST fakes) and FakeBackend'

findings.finding: The plan maps 'IdentityConvention ← rebar_id_audit.py', but rebar_id_audit.py is an authorization guard (blocks unauthorized label writes), not a stamp/find interface. The actual stamp (write rebar-id label to Jira) lives in apply_inbound.py/apply_inbound_records.py; the find (read rebar-id from a Jira payload) lives in inbound_translate.py and binding_walk.py. Building IdentityConvention by wrapping only rebar_id_audit.py will produce an interface missing the actual stamp and find methods the contract test expects.
findings.location: Approach section: 'IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention (rebar_id_audit.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Approach bullet with: 'IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label stamp/find surface: `stamp` wraps the label-write helpers in `apply_inbound_records.py`; `find` wraps the label-parse helpers in `inbound_translate.py`/`binding_walk.py`. `rebar_id_audit.py` is the authorization guard (not the convention), and may be invoked internally by JiraBackend.identity_convention.stamp but is not itself the interface.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
